segments.title: What the Royal Navy expected war games to teach
segments.summary: The 1929 rules connected tabletop decisions to fleet exercises, common doctrine, decentralized command, and readiness for a war that might begin anywhere.
segments.body: The Admiralty's Tactical Division issued a common framework that could be used from individual-ship drills through fleet exercises. Its stated goals included teaching the battle plan, building common tactical doctrine, testing new ideas and material, and encouraging subordinate initiative. Officers also had to learn the capabilities of foreign ships and apply realistic navigation errors to aircraft operating as independent formations. Because war might begin anywhere and bring together ships from different stations at short notice, shared methods were essential to making an improvised force work together. The rules therefore linked tabletop decisions, staff training, and practical fleet exercises into one system of readiness.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces ADM 186/78, its authorship, scope, and tactical training objectives.

segments.title: Cruiser problems disguised the practice of economic war
segments.summary: Cruiser exercises trained scouting, patrol, and commerce-war skills while also rehearsing chart work, communications, command relationships, and war diaries.
segments.body: The politically awkward subject of surface raiding could be taught under the safer label of a cruiser problem. Exercise rules demanded realistic movements, fuel and endurance calculations, accurate plotting of friendly forces, disciplined command relationships, and written narratives. Junior officers could temporarily receive senior game commands, forcing everyone to respect the exercise chain of command and giving promising officers a demanding test. Clarke links this attention to staff work and reporting with the Royal Navy's effort to avoid a repeat of the communication failures associated with Jutland. The watch point shows how an apparently tactical game also rehearsed the administrative habits needed to command at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript explains cruiser problems, chart work, command roles, Jutland, and exercise narratives.

segments.title: Turning gunfire and torpedo hits into operational damage
segments.summary: Detailed tables adjusted peacetime firing results for combat and translated hits into lost speed or fighting efficiency rather than a simple sunk-or-survived result.
segments.body: The rules reduced peacetime gunnery performance by 30 percent to represent battle conditions, then applied factors for target size, visibility, smoke, air spotting, and return fire. Tables distinguished older and newer battleships, alternative gun arrangements, anti-aircraft weapons, and the cumulative effect of non-vital hits. Torpedo effects varied with warhead and hit location, with damage to rudders, shafts, and propellers capable of slowing an entire formation. Umpires could impose either reduced speed or reduced fighting efficiency, making the operational consequence depend on what was damaged rather than on a simple sunk-or-survived result. This made fleet maneuver, formation speed, and the ability to continue a battle part of damage assessment.

evidence.note: The transcript details gunnery factors, torpedo tables, speed reduction, and fighting efficiency.

segments.title: Periscope visibility and serviceability constrained reconnaissance
segments.summary: The rules treated submarine sighting distance and the declining serviceability of aircraft as operational limits rather than abstract background details.
segments.body: Submarine visibility tables varied the distance at which ships and destroyers could be seen through a periscope according to sea state and the proximity of the enemy. The model forced officers to consider how long and how high a periscope could safely be exposed. Aircraft calculations began with 90 percent serviceability, fell to 80 percent on the second day, and used 70 percent thereafter during sustained operations. Worked examples converted those percentages into the number of floatplanes that could remain continuously aloft. This watch point shows reconnaissance being constrained by equipment availability, observation conditions, and endurance rather than granted automatically.

evidence.note: The transcript explains periscope visibility ranges and declining aircraft serviceability during sustained operations.

segments.title: Carrier deck capacity turned fleet aviation into a timing problem
segments.summary: Deck space, lifts, hangars, engine warm-up, and foreign carrier comparisons determined how quickly an air group could become a usable striking force.
segments.body: The rules specified how many large aircraft, fighters, or reconnaissance machines each British carrier could range on its decks. Lift arrangements and hangar position changed the time needed to bring aircraft up, while water-cooled and radial engines required different warm-up allowances. Foreign carriers were mapped to British comparison types: Hosho to Hermes, and Kaga, Lexington, and Saratoga to Furious. The rules even credited the American carriers with the ability to range aircraft forward while preserving landing space aft. The detail reveals that carrier strength was measured not only by aircraft totals but by the tempo at which a ship could launch and recover them.

evidence.note: The transcript lists British deck capacities, foreign-carrier comparison types, lift timings, and engine warm-up allowances.

segments.title: Air attack tables connected weapons effects to operational range
segments.summary: Torpedo-hit probabilities, bombing depth limits, fuel reserves, wind, and recovery delays made naval air strikes a combined weapons-and-endurance calculation.
segments.body: The rules estimated aerial torpedo hits by formation size, target type, maneuver, and the possibility of surprise. Battleships and carriers were more suitable targets than cruisers or destroyers, while bombing tables sharply reduced an aircraft's chance of damaging a deeply submerged submarine. Radius of action meant the greatest straight-line distance an aircraft could fly while retaining enough fuel to return, not its theoretical maximum range. Fuel capacity, airspeed, time over the objective, wind, launch delays, landing delays, and emergency reserves all entered the calculation. Clarke argues that this complexity gave more officers realistic work and helped connect a local fleet exercise to the demands of a wider war. The damage model also encouraged carrier aircraft to slow enemy formations so the battle fleet could bring them to action.

evidence.note: The transcript covers aerial torpedo probabilities, bombing against submarines, radius of action, fuel margins, and formation-speed damage.

segments.title: Communications rules prepared fleets for dispersed warfare
segments.summary: Signal delays and traffic priorities were built into games because carriers and night operations were spreading actions across much wider areas.
segments.body: Wireless messages were assigned realistic transmission times that grew with traffic and priority demands. Maneuver signals, first contact reports, later reports, and outgoing admiral's signals could therefore reach recipients at different speeds. The Royal Navy was anticipating combat dispersed both globally and tactically, with aviation extending the battlefield and darkness increasing uncertainty. Staffs had to decide what deserved priority while avoiding congestion that could delay a commander's plan. Repeated practice helped officers exchange necessary information under pressure, even if wartime signals would never be perfect or instantaneous.

evidence.note: The transcript explains wireless timing, traffic priority, dispersed action, and night-fighting complexity.

segments.title: Exercise MZ tested air attack in a balanced fleet problem
segments.summary: Exercise MZ matched carrier-supported fleets in a Mediterranean problem and used the new rules to translate air attacks into lost battle-fleet speed.
segments.body: MZ pitted the Mediterranean and Atlantic Fleets against one another, with ships representing additional battleships and battlecruisers that were not physically present. Clarke reads the stronger red force as an approximation of a likely Royal Navy task group and the blue force as a possible Japanese opponent, though British battlecruisers stood in for foreign fast heavy ships. Both sides fielded carriers, submarines, reconnaissance aircraft, fighters, and torpedo bombers. Ten aircraft from Courageous were assessed to hit Nelson twice and Rodney once, reducing the squadron to eleven knots. Because the March exercise used an early copy of the rules published in April, it also acted as a practical test of the damage model before final issue.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces MZ's represented forces, possible Japanese comparison, carrier aircraft, and assessed attacks on Nelson and Rodney.

segments.title: Air reconnaissance freed cruisers but exposed the carriers
segments.summary: MZ showed that aircraft could replace cruiser scouting, allowing cruisers to raid the enemy screen or carriers while exposing the weakness of carrier protection.
segments.body: Frobisher was put out of action while confirming an air report that had already located the enemy battle fleet. The exercise report asked whether cruisers could instead attack carriers, dominate the area ahead of the fleet, support friendly carriers, or strike destroyer flotillas. This new freedom also revealed that the destroyers near a carrier were often plane guards rather than a security screen capable of stopping hostile cruisers. The lesson anticipated the danger later demonstrated by the wartime losses of Courageous and Glorious when operational circumstances left them inadequately protected. MZ also criticized the dispersal of surface and aerial torpedo attacks across too many objectives. Clarke places the exercise in the Mediterranean, where Britain combined fleet training with knowledge of a vital route to the Far East and a visible demonstration of naval strength.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Frobisher, alternative cruiser tasks, carrier vulnerability, dispersed torpedo attacks, and Mediterranean strategic geography.

segments.title: Exercise M1 tested concentrated carrier torpedo strikes
segments.summary: Exercise M1 linked the speed needed for flying operations to early warning, fighter patrols, and the duty of the whole fleet to protect its carriers.
segments.body: M1 brought together Dart torpedo bombers, Fairey reconnaissance aircraft, Flycatcher fighters, and carriers with different normal speeds. Flying operations required the carriers to accelerate to about twenty-five knots, making their movement part of the tactical problem. The report endorsed the earliest possible warning of hostile surface forces and considered dedicated aircraft for that purpose. It also made senior officers across the fleet responsible for knowing the carriers' position and heading off enemy forces trying to work around the battle line. Air fighting in MZ encouraged further experiments with fighter patrols over the fleet, so carrier defense was becoming a combined responsibility rather than a matter for the carrier alone.

evidence.note: The transcript describes M1's aircraft, carrier speed for flight operations, early warning, carrier security, and fighter patrols.

segments.title: M1 compared torpedo performance with strike concentration
segments.summary: Reports from M1 weighed equipment reliability, pilot practice, fighter escort, and the delay required to combine several carriers' torpedo bombers into one attack.
segments.body: Short-range torpedo aiming depended heavily on pilot judgment, while differences in torpedo reliability and practice helped explain the contrasting results of the Mediterranean and Atlantic Fleets. Senior officers argued that a concerted attack by all available torpedo bombers should be more effective than separate attacks from each carrier. Concentration, however, imposed a delay and became harder to control at night, when several smaller formations might be more manageable than one mass. The exercise also tested how many fighters should remain over the fleet and how many should escort the striking force. Some officers expected the rear guns of two-seat torpedo bombers to reduce the need for fighter protection, revealing a doctrinal assumption that later combat would challenge.

evidence.note: The transcript compares torpedo sights, weapon reliability, pilot practice, massed attacks, night control, and fighter escort.

segments.title: Exercise OA showed how submarines could overload a fleet
segments.summary: OA tested a mixed battleship-and-battlecruiser force, divided destroyer screens, and the disruption caused when rescue duties removed ships from the battle.
segments.body: OA began with a Gibraltar relief setting and asked how a battleship force should fight an opponent mixing battleships and battlecruisers. It also tested whether destroyer flotillas should be divided into groups on each wing of the approaching battle fleet. Cruisers made contact under heavy pressure, while destroyers altered course to reach a favorable attacking position. A reported downed reconnaissance aircraft drew Argus and the First Flotilla away to rescue the aircrew and salvage the machine. Neither could rejoin before the action, demonstrating how an essential rescue could abruptly change the force available for a planned battle.

evidence.note: The transcript sets out OA's Gibraltar scenario, mixed heavy forces, destroyer-wing experiment, cruiser action, and rescue diversion.

segments.title: K26 obeyed its target priority and passed up Hood
segments.summary: OA's most striking command problem came when K26 declined a close shot at Hood because its assigned objective was the enemy battle fleet.
segments.body: K26 reached a position from which it could have fired at Hood from about 3,500 yards. The submarine did not attack because its orders prioritized the enemy battle fleet, then had to dive deep as Hood closed and took no further part in the exercise. The episode exposed the cost of rigid target priorities when an unexpected high-value opportunity appeared. The surface battle meanwhile reduced both fleets severely, with Red winning only after suffering substantial damage and speed loss. OA therefore offered no tidy victory narrative: it showed how orders, maneuver, torpedo fire, and accumulated damage could leave even the winning fleet badly constrained.

evidence.note: The transcript follows K26's missed opportunity against Hood and summarizes the heavily damaged fleets at the exercise's end.

segments.title: Submarines overloaded the fleet before they scored a hit
segments.summary: OA measured sustained flying and ASW patrols, then concluded that submarine pressure could divert forces, congest signals, and delay a fleet commander's decisions.
segments.body: The exercise recorded reconnaissance, fighter, and torpedo-bomber strength as well as flying hours, deck landings, forced landings, and the duration of individual sorties. Eagle maintained a continuous anti-submarine patrol, showing that submarine pressure consumed air effort even before a successful attack. The Admiralty concluded that fleet submarines forced aircraft and light forces away from their main tasks, congested communications, complicated the withdrawal of the ASW screen, and could delay a favorable attack. They also threatened to reduce battle-fleet speed or damage valuable advanced units such as battlecruisers and carriers. Clarke interprets this as operational overload: enough reports and competing dangers could overwhelm a commander whose staff was not trained to filter and act on them.

evidence.note: The transcript gives OA's air activity and Admiralty conclusions about submarine-driven diversion, congestion, delay, and speed loss.

segments.title: Exercise OD rehearsed convoy interception and night battle
segments.summary: OD embedded a protected convoy in a Mediterranean land campaign, linking submarine attacks on communications to the urgent need for reinforcements and stores at Derna.
segments.body: OD gave a weaker but faster force the task of attacking a protected convoy while avoiding the enemy's superior battle strength. The scenario placed bases at Malta and Port Said and made Derna an advanced base under siege by red land forces. Blue submarine attacks on red communications had left the besieging army urgently short of reinforcements and stores, so the convoy's passage had a direct effect ashore. Ships represented absent battlecruisers and other units, allowing the exercise to model a larger operational force than the fleet physically available. The open-ended format let the commander-in-chief continue the problem until the participants had extracted its useful lessons.

evidence.note: The transcript lays out OD's forces, protected-convoy problem, bases, submarine pressure on communications, and Derna supply crisis.

segments.title: OD used air search to set up a night interception
segments.summary: Blue planned to locate and shadow the convoy with aircraft and submarines, refuse battle by day, and use darkness to offset the enemy's superior strength.
segments.body: Blue spread submarines toward Malta and ordered Courageous to search up to eighty miles while maintaining visual contact with the battlecruiser Revenge. Once the convoy was located, aircraft and submarines were to shadow it and help the faster force avoid a superior opponent in daylight. The intended solution was a night attack, drawing on the lesson of Jutland that darkness could help a weaker force escape or reverse a numerical disadvantage. The exercise produced complicated night encounters involving battlecruisers and destroyers between 00:30 and 02:20. Its conclusions called for continued heavy-ship night training and a reliable system of recognition signals, because tactical aggression was useless if formations could not identify one another after dark.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Blue's air and submarine search, daylight avoidance, night encounters, and recognition-signal lesson.

segments.title: Exercise OC linked submarine shadowing with air and ASW screens
segments.summary: OC asked a submarine line to intercept, shadow, and report a fleet so another force could bring it to action.
segments.body: OC used L-class submarines to represent a G-class flotilla operating from Philippeville. Intelligence about a damaged battlecruiser drew the opposing fleet to sea, giving the submarines an opportunity to form an interception line. Their orders were to locate, shadow, and report the fleet so Red could bring it to action rather than merely attack whatever appeared first. Courageous and its attendant destroyers operated with the battle squadron, making air cooperation and carrier positioning part of the same reconnaissance problem. The setup shows submarines acting as fleet scouts and information providers, not only as independent torpedo attackers.

evidence.note: The transcript describes OC's represented submarine flotilla, intelligence scenario, interception line, shadowing orders, and carrier-supported battle squadron.

segments.title: OC opened a wider problem of air-ASW cooperation
segments.summary: Debate after OC explored aircraft-guided destroyer sweeps, breaking submarine contact at night, and the carrier's safe place behind an ASDIC screen.
segments.body: The commanding officer of Courageous proposed combining spotter-reconnaissance aircraft with destroyers to prevent submarines from maintaining contact. A night sweep followed by a bold change of course might break the shadowing line, while further trials could compare destroyer groups operating with and without aircraft. The discussion expanded into coordination between aircraft and ASDIC vessels, emergency procedures for forced landings near submarines, and whether a carrier could maneuver safely in the triangle behind the screen. Senior officers disagreed about what could be tested without endangering submarines or distorting the exercise, and the commander-in-chief warned against drawing universal conclusions from a carefully arranged set piece. Clarke connects the growing complexity of carrier employment to the Royal Navy's creation of a Rear Admiral Aircraft Carriers appointment in 1930.

evidence.note: The transcript debates aircraft-destroyer cooperation, night contact-breaking, ASDIC screens, carrier safety, exercise limits, and carrier command.

segments.title: The 1929 exercises found questions faster than answers
segments.summary: The closing assessment presents the exercises as a learning system that exposed uncertainty and demanded improvement a decade before the Second World War.
segments.body: The Royal Navy was synthesizing lessons from the 1920s while recognizing that aviation, submarines, communications, and night operations had changed the problem inherited from the First World War. Exercises produced more questions and improvement points than grounds for confidence, even in areas such as night fighting where the service compared well with other navies. That willingness to expose uncertainty mattered because the fleet still had a decade to refine doctrine before the Second World War. Clarke's central judgment is that the service benchmarked itself against whichever navy or formation represented the highest capability. It then demanded further progress from officers and formations rather than accepting competence as good enough.

evidence.note: The transcript summarizes the exercises as preparation, experimentation, and continual self-comparison.
